passage: Indeed, Abū Bakr repeated his noble action several times. ʿĀmir ibn Fuhayrah was a slave who belonged to al- Ṭufayl ibn ʿ Abdullāh al-Asdī , who was related to Abū Bakr through his wife, Umm Rūman, ʿĀ ’ishah’s mother. ʿĀmir ibn Fuhayrah was among the very early Muslims. For this, he suffered a great deal; he was one of the victims of the campaign of terror. Abū Bakr, however, bought him from his master and set him free. ʿĀmir continued to work for Abū Bakr as a shepherd. Later he was to play an important role in helping the Prophet and Abū Bakr to emigrate from Makkah to Madinah. 3 The Prophet was very pleased with Abū Bakr’s action with those two slaves. He encouraged him to do what he could in order to help those victims of the campaign of torture. Abū Bakr responded, as he always did, without any hesitation. He chose the most helpless and those who showed a great determination to defy the prosecutors. Zunayrah was a slave who belonged to the Makhzūm clan. As such, it was left to Abū Jahl to torture her. He gave her so much of his venom that she lost her sight. Then he told her: “It is the two gods al-Lāt and al- ʿ Uzzāwho have done this to you.” She replied: “How can al-Lāt and al- ʿ Uzzāknow who worship them? This is simply the will of God and my Lord is able to give me back my sight.” When she woke up the following day, she had regained her sight. People of the Quraysh said: “This is part of Muhammad’s magic.” Abū Bakr moved swiftly to rescue this woman slave and set her free. 4 A woman called al-Nahdiyah and her daughter were slaves belonging to a woman from the clan of ʿ Abd al-Dār. Both became Muslims and their mistress ordered them to be tortured. One day she gave them a quantity of flour to bake, but as she set them to their task, she threatened them: “You will remain my slaves for ever. By God, I will never set you free.” Abū Bakr was nearby, and he heard the woman. He said to her: “Why don’t you release yourself from your oath?” She replied, “Release myself! It was you who spoiled them, so you set them free if you wish.” Abū Bakr asked her what price she wanted for them, and he paid her the price. Immediately, he declared that they were free and told the two women to give their former mistress her flour. They asked him whether he would mind if they finished it for her. He said that was up to them. 5 Another woman slave, called Umm ʿ Ubays, belonged to al-Aswad ibn ʿ Abd Yaghūth from the clan of Zuhrah. As Abū Bakr saw her one day being tortured by her master, he bought her and set her free. He did the same with a woman slave who belonged to the clan of ʿ Adiy, in which ʿ Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb was the tormentor of Muslims as he was still an unbeliever then. ʿ Umar used to beat up this woman as frequently and as painfully as he could. One day he stopped beating her, saying: “I apologize to you! I am only stopping beating you up because I am bored.” She said, “It is God who does this to you.” Then Abū Bakr bought her and set her free. 6 All in all Abū Bakr freed seven slaves to save them from the campaign of terror launched by the Quraysh. His father, who was not a Muslim, could not understand his action. He said to him: “Son, I see that you are setting free weak slaves. If you want to do this, why don’t you set free some strong men slaves who will be able to protect you?” Abū Bakr explained that he wanted only to be rewarded by God for his action. 7 Abū Bakr’s action was the best example of how closely knit the new Muslim community was. Those who were able to help their brothers and sisters did not hesitate to render any assistance they could. Nevertheless, Abū Bakr’s actions were exemplary. Neither he nor the other Muslims who enjoyed tribal lineage were able to stop the campaign of terror. Although he was reasonably wealthy, he could not buy all the victims of the Quraysh’s persecution. Not all of them were slaves anyway, and those who were could not have been bought because their masters refused to sell them. Abū Bakr’s action demonstrated the new bond that was established among the followers of the new religion. There was another aspect to what he did: it emphasized that the followers of the new religion were all equal, slaves and masters alike. Much later, when ʿ Umar became one of the leading figures among the Muslims, he stated this fact in the clearest of terms when he said of Abū Bakr and what he did for Bilāl: “Abū Bakr is our master, and he has freed our master.” 8
source: Adil Salahi, Muhammad: Man and Prophet, p. 65

story_context.introduction: Once upon a time, in a city called Makkah, there lived a kind and brave man named Abu Bakr. He loved helping those in need and standing up for what was right.
story_context.conclusion: Abu Bakr's selfless actions inspired others to do the same. He showed everyone that helping others is a great way to please God and make the world a better place.
story_context.moral_lesson: We should always try to help those who are less fortunate than us, just like Abu Bakr did.

units.unit_text: Indeed, Abū Bakr repeated his noble action several times. ʿĀmir ibn Fuhayrah was a slave who belonged to al- Ṭufayl ibn ʿ Abdullāh al-Asdī , who was related to Abū Bakr through his wife, Umm Rūman, ʿĀ ’ishah’s mother.
main_characters: al- Ṭufayl ibn ʿ Abdullāh al-Asdī
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr bought ʿĀmir ibn Fuhayrah from his master and set him free.

units.unit_text: Later he was to play an important role in helping the Prophet and Abū Bakr to emigrate from Makkah to Madinah.
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr helped the Prophet and himself to emigrate from Makkah to Madinah.

units.unit_text: Zunayrah was a slave who belonged to the Makhzūm clan. As such, it was left to Abū Jahl to torture her. He gave her so much of his venom that she lost her sight.
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr rescued Zunayrah from torture and set her free.

units.unit_title: Abū Bakr Rescues Al-Nahdiyah and Her Daughter
units.unit_text: A woman called al-Nahdiyah and her daughter were slaves belonging to a woman from the clan of ʿ Abd al-Dār. Both became Muslims and their mistress ordered them to be tortured.
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr bought al-Nahdiyah and her daughter from their mistress and set them free.

units.unit_title: Abū Bakr Frees Umm ʿ Ubays
units.unit_text: Another woman slave, called Umm ʿ Ubays, belonged to al-Aswad ibn ʿ Abd Yaghūth from the clan of Zuhrah. As Abū Bakr saw her one day being tortured by her master, he bought her and set her free.
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr bought Umm ʿ Ubays from her master and set her free.

units.unit_text: He did the same with a woman slave who belonged to the clan of ʿ Adiy, in which ʿ Umar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb was the tormentor of Muslims as he was still an unbeliever then.
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr bought a slave from the clan of ʿ Adiy and set her free.

units.unit_text: His father, who was not a Muslim, could not understand his action. He said to him: “Son, I see that you are setting free weak slaves. If you want to do this, why don’t you set free some strong men slaves who will be able to protect you?”
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr's father disapproved of his actions.

units.unit_text: Abū Bakr’s action demonstrated the new bond that was established among the followers of the new religion. There was another aspect to what he did: it emphasized that the followers of the new religion were all equal, slaves and masters alike.
units.event_summary: Abū Bakr's actions showed the equality of followers in the new religion.

units.unit_title: ʿ Umar's Praise for Abū Bakr
units.unit_text: Much later, when ʿ Umar became one of the leading figures among the Muslims, he stated this fact in the clearest of terms when he said of Abū Bakr and what he did for Bilāl: “Abū Bakr is our master, and he has freed our master.”
units.event_summary: ʿ Umar praised Abū Bakr for his actions.

scene_input.scene_summary: Abu Bakr learns about 'Amir ibn Fuhayrah's enslavement
scene_input.key_visual_action: Abu Bakr reading a message or hearing news
scene_input.setting: Abu Bakr's home or a public gathering place

panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr was deeply concerned when he heard the news about Amir ibn Fuhayrah's enslavement.
panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor storybook illustration, soft warm hand-painted style for ages 6-12: The scene unfolds in a serene, 7th century Arabian home, bathed in warm sunlight streaming through the high windows. The central figure, Abu Bakr, sits on a plush cushion, his medium build and dignified posture exuding confidence and wisdom. He wears a flowing white thobe, its fabric softly draped around his body, and a deep green woolen cloak draped majestically over both shoulders. A white turban adorns his head, with a cloth tail hanging elegantly between the shoulder blades.

Abu Bakr's face is obscured, but his expression conveys deep concern and compassion. A gentle furrow creases his brow, and his eyes seem to hold a world of understanding and empathy. A messenger stands before him, a young man with a simple, yet dignified, attire of beige and white. The messenger's hands are clasped together, and his head is bowed in respect, as he shares the news or message with Abu Bakr.

The room is sparsely furnished, with a few plush cushions and a low, intricately carved table. The walls are adorned with beautiful, intricate Islamic geometric patterns, their delicate lines and shapes dancing across the walls in soft, golden hues. The dominant color of the scene is a soft, warm beige, with accents of deep green and white. The soft warm sunlight streaming through the windows casts a gentle glow on the scene, illuminating the textures and patterns of the fabrics and the intricate designs on the walls.

The composition is a gentle, intimate shot, focusing on the emotional connection between Abu Bakr and the messenger. The shot is taken from a slight angle, with the camera positioned slightly above Abu Bakr's shoulder, looking down at the scene. The warm, soft colors and gentle lighting create a sense of calm and serenity, inviting the viewer to step into the peaceful world of 7th century Arabia.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.image_prompt:
A children's watercolor storybook illustration, soft warm hand-painted style for ages 6-12. A bustling marketplace or public meeting place in early Makkah, with the warm glow of the Arabian sun casting long shadows between the palm trees, mud-brick buildings, and simple furnishings. The scene is filled with the sounds of merchants haggling over goods and the smell of fresh bread wafting from a nearby oven.

In the center of the scene, the central figure, a medium build man with a dignified upright posture, is seen from behind, wearing a flowing white thobe with a deep green woolen cloak draped over both shoulders and a white turban with a cloth tail hanging between the shoulder blades, is handing over something to al-Tufayl ibn Abdullah, a medium build man wearing a simple brown thobe with a plain white turban. The central figure's gentle gesture conveys a sense of kindness and generosity. Al-Hafayl's posture is one of respect, his head slightly bowed as he receives the item.

Nearby, a young man, of medium build, stands with a look of curiosity on his face, wearing a plain brown thobe with a plain white cloth tail hanging between the shoulder blades. His eyes are fixed on the central figure, taking in the scene unfolding before him.

In the background, a young woman, seen from behind, is standing modestly dressed in traditional clothing, her jilbab a deep shade of indigo and her khimar a soft white. She is watching the scene with a gentle smile, her hands clasped together in front of her.

On a nearby mat, a swaddled bundle lies peacefully, a reminder of the new life that has been brought into this community. The atmosphere is joyful, with warm natural light casting a gentle glow on the scene. The composition is a medium shot, straight on, inviting the viewer to step into the world of early Makkah.

Color palette: warm earth tones, soft pastels, and the deep green of the woolen cloak. Lighting: soft, warm natural light casting a gentle glow on the scene, with the sun shining down from above. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

scene_input.scene_summary: Abu Bakr sets 'Amir ibn Fuhayrah free
scene_input.key_visual_action: Abu Bakr releasing 'Amir ibn Fuhayrah from a rope or a chain
scene_input.setting: a public place, such as a square or a park

panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr stood tall, his eyes shining with determination as he approached Amir ibn Fuhayrah. With a gentle yet firm gesture, he released the rope that bound his friend, setting him free.
panels.moral_lesson: The importance of setting free the oppressed and standing up against injustice
panels.image_prompt:
A serene 7th century Arabian public place, such as a charming square or a lush park, is the backdrop for this tender scene. Soft, warm watercolor hues evoke a sense of tranquility, reminiscent of a gentle desert breeze. The sky above is a brilliant blue, with a few wispy clouds drifting lazily across it. In the distance, a few majestic palm trees stand tall, their leafy fronds swaying gently in the breeze. A simple stone fountain, its surface adorned with intricate patterns, provides a soothing background melody. The warm natural light, with its soft golden quality, casts a gentle shadow on the ground, adding depth and dimension to the scene.

In the foreground, the rope that binds Amir ibn Fuhayrah, a slender young man, is prominently displayed. His simple brown woolen cloak, with a white cloth head covering, is a testament to his modesty and humility. The rope, worn and weathered from repeated use, is a symbol of the young man's predicament.

Standing opposite Amir, Abu Bakr, the central figure seen from behind, exudes an air of dignity and compassion. His medium build and upright posture command respect, as he stands tall in his flowing white thobe. The deep green woolen cloak draped over his shoulders adds a pop of color to the scene, while the white turban, with its cloth tail hanging between the shoulder blades, completes his traditional Arabian attire. His hand, extended in a gentle yet firm gesture, conveys a sense of reassurance and guidance.

The medium shot composition, with the central figure and the young man shown in profile, allows the viewer to focus on the emotional connection between the two characters. The warm golden light, with its deep green, white, and brown color palette, creates a sense of harmony and balance in the scene. As the viewer's eye is drawn to the rope, then to Abu Bakr's extended hand, they are invited to reflect on the themes of compassion, forgiveness, and redemption. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr and Amir ibn Fuhayrah met and became fast friends, united by their love of the Prophet and their desire to serve Allah.
panels.moral_lesson: The importance of friendship and kindness
panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor illustration, soft warm hand-painted style for ages 6-12. Setting: The interior of a traditional 7th century Arabian home, with a few plush pillows and a low table adorned with intricately woven textiles. The warm and cozy space is illuminated by the soft, golden light pouring in through a window, casting a gentle glow on the characters.

In the center of the illustration, the central figure (depiction: NO_FACE), Abu Bakr, stands with a dignified upright posture, his medium build radiating confidence. He wears a flowing white thobe with a distinctive deep green woolen cloak draped elegantly over both shoulders, framing his majestic presence. A white turban sits atop his head, complete with a cloth tail hanging between the shoulder blades, adding a touch of traditional elegance.

Amir ibn Fuhayrah, a slender-built companion, stands beside him, a gentle smile on his face (depiction: NO_FACE). His simple white thobe is draped over his lithe frame, complemented by a plain brown woolen cloak and a white cloth head covering, exuding a sense of humility and kindness.

The two friends are engaged in a warm and friendly gesture, their hands clasped together in a gentle handshake or a smile that speaks volumes about their deep affection for one another. The soft light of the window highlights the joy and camaraderie between them, creating a sense of warmth and intimacy.

The color palette features deep green, brown, white, and off-white hues, evoking the rich textures and earthy tones of 7th century Arabia. The composition is a gentle, warm handshake or smile between the two friends, inviting the viewer to join in their celebration of friendship and loyalty.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.image_prompt:
Watercolor illustration in a soft, warm hand-painted style, reminiscent of traditional storybooks, suitable for children aged 6-12. The scene captures a gentle moment in a simple house in Makkah, circa 7th century Arabia. The walls are made of rough-hewn stone, adorned with intricate patterns of geometric shapes and vines, characteristic of early Islamic architecture. A few furnishings, such as a low wooden bench and a woven reed mat, add to the cozy atmosphere.

In the foreground, Abu Bakr stands with a dignified, medium build, and an upright posture, his plain brown woolen cloak draped over his shoulder, and a simple white thobe that reaches down to his ankles. His white cloth head covering is tied neatly under his chin, concealing his face. His expression is one of concern and compassion as he engages in a heartfelt conversation with the central figure, whose presence is felt through his gentle, soothing words.

The central figure, seen from behind, exudes an aura of calm and wisdom. He wears a flowing white thobe, its hem delicately embroidered with intricate patterns, and a deep green woolen cloak draped elegantly over both shoulders. His white cloth head covering is tied similarly to Abu Bakr's, adding to the sense of shared tradition and respect.

Soft, warm light filters in through a small window, casting a gentle glow on the scene. The source of light is from the left, illuminating the faces of Abu Bakr and the central figure, while casting a soft shadow on the wall behind them. The gentle close-up shot emphasizes the emotional tone of the scene, drawing the viewer's attention to the concern and compassion etched on Abu Bakr's face.

The color palette is predominantly soft blues and whites, evoking a sense of serenity and tranquility. Accents of deep green add a touch of warmth and vibrancy to the scene, echoing the colors of the central figure's cloak. No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr and the Prophet rode their camels into Madinah, where they were greeted with joy by the people.
panels.moral_lesson: The importance of community and welcome
panels.image_prompt:
A serene watercolor scene unfolds in the streets of Madinah, with the warm glow of a sunny day casting a soft light upon the characters. The central figure of the man in the green cloak, seen from behind, sits majestically on a majestic camel, its coat a rich chestnut brown, adorned with intricately woven saddlebags made from supple leather. His flowing green cloak, a vibrant contrast to the earthy tones of the surroundings, gently flows behind him, while his white turban is tied securely around his head, and a cloth tail hangs elegantly between his shoulder blades.

By his side, Abu Bakr rides a sturdy camel, its coat a lighter beige, with a dignified upright posture, exuding confidence and poise. He wears a simple white thobe, its fabric smooth and unadorned, over a plain brown woolen cloak that falls in soft folds around his body. A white cloth head covering, neatly tied, adds a touch of humility to his overall demeanor.

In the background, a lively scene unfolds as people of Madinah, dressed in simple white and brown clothing, wave and smile in greeting. A young woman, wrapped in a jilbab, her khimar modestly covering her hair, holds a swaddled bundle in her arms, her face radiant with joy. The scene is set against a bright blue sky, with a few puffy white clouds drifting lazily across the horizon.

The art style is reminiscent of a soft, hand-painted watercolor, with delicate brushstrokes and subtle color gradations that evoke a sense of warmth and tranquility. The composition is a masterful blend of foreground and background, drawing the viewer's eye to the central figures, while the surrounding crowd adds a sense of energy and movement to the scene.

No modern objects, historically accurate 7th century scene.

scene_input.scene_summary: Abu Bakr frees Zunayrah from her chains

panels.narrative_text: Abu Bakr saw Zunayrah being mistreated and decided to take action. He bravely approached the Makhzum clan and freed her from her chains, giving her the gift of freedom.
panels.moral_lesson: The importance of compassion and freedom
panels.image_prompt:
Illustration of Abu Bakr breaking the chains that bind Zunayrah, set against a warm desert landscape. The art style is a soft, watercolor storybook style, reminiscent of hand-painted illustrations, with a focus on gentle colors and delicate textures. The scene is bathed in the warm glow of the sun, casting long shadows across the sand and accentuating the beauty of the surrounding palm trees and distant mountain range.

Abu Bakr, the central figure seen from behind, stands tall with a dignified upright posture, his medium build radiating a sense of strength and purpose. He is dressed in a flowing white thobe, cinched at the waist with a simple brown leather belt, and draped in a plain brown woolen cloak. On his head, a white cloth head covering is wrapped neatly, a symbol of his devotion to his faith. His action is the focal point of the illustration, as he grasps the chains that bind Zunayrah and begins to break them apart, his muscles flexing with effort.

Zunayrah, a young woman of slender build, stands behind Abu Bakr, her worn and tattered dark brown jilbab a testament to her hardships. A white cloth head covering, slightly disheveled, frames her face, and chains bind her wrists and ankles, a poignant reminder of her captivity. Yet, as Abu Bakr frees her, her posture and expression convey a sense of joy and liberation, her shoulders relaxing and a hint of a smile playing on her lips.

The lighting in the scene is warm and golden, with the sun shining down from the top left corner, casting a sense of hope and redemption over the image. The composition is a dynamic shot, with Abu Bakr's action and Zunayrah's freedom as the central focus, set against the serene backdrop of the desert landscape.

The color palette is muted, with earthy tones of beige, sienna, and umber, punctuated by the white of Abu Bakr's thobe and the dark brown of Zunayrah's jilbab. The overall effect is one of serenity and hope, a testament to the power of freedom and redemption in a beautiful, historically accurate 7th century scene.
